did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for real-time skin cancer classification (melanoma detection). The model was trained on the ISIC 2024 dataset using transfer learning and deployed on a Raspberry Pi edge device, achieving 96.4% accuracy and 98.5% recall.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design, implementation, and validation of an AI and IoT system for skin cancer detection. It details a specific methodology (MobileNetV2 on Raspberry Pi), reports original experimental results (96.4% accuracy, 98.5% recall), and includes a discussion of findings and limitations, which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review.

title: Integrating AI and IoT for the Implementation of Skin Cancer Detection in Hospital 4.0
journal: 2025 International Conference on Circuit, Systems and Communication (ICCSC)
brief_summary: This study proposes an AI and IoT system for real-time skin cancer detection, specifically melanoma classification, within a Hospital 4.0 framework. The researchers implemented a lightweight MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network on a Raspberry Pi edge device, trained using transfer learning on the ISIC 2024 dataset. The system was integrated with a Flask-based web application to facilitate secure data transmission and Electronic Health Record interoperability. Evaluation metrics demonstrated high diagnostic performance, achieving 96.4% accuracy and 98.5% recall on the test set. Additionally, real-time inference testing on a benign skin anomaly confirmed the system's ability to correctly identify non-cancerous lesions with low confidence scores. The results indicate that this edge-AI solution offers a cost-effective, scalable, and privacy-preserving approach for early dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant). The model was trained on the ISIC 2024 dataset which contains images categorized as benign and malignant diseases. The system achieved 98.5% recall for melanoma detection.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The dataset included benign diseases. A specific real-time inference test was performed on a 'benign skin anomaly (a pimple)' which resulted in a low-confidence melanoma prediction (6.13%), confirming the system's ability to identify non-cancerous lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the system performs 'real-time skin cancer classification' and 'image classification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant/melanoma) using a MobileNetV2 CNN trained on the ISIC 2024 dataset. The loss function used was 'Categorical cross-entropy loss for multi-class classification' (though the context implies a binary task of benign vs malignant, the architecture supports classification). Metrics reported include Accuracy (96.4%), Recall (98.5%), Precision, F1-Score, and ROC-AUC (97.4%), which are standard for classification tasks. The output includes class labels and confidence scores (e.g., 'Melanoma (Confidence: 6.13%)').

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024 dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly disclosed dermatological dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing a wide variety of skin lesion images categorized as benign and malignant diseases. It was used as the primary dataset for training the MobileNetV2 model via transfer learning.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV2 model using transfer learning. The final classification layers were trained on this dataset. 20% of the dataset was reserved for validation during training.
use.validation: 20% of the dataset was reserved for validation to monitor loss and apply early stopping.
use.testing: The model's performance was evaluated on a test set derived from this data, achieving 96.4% accuracy and 98.5% recall.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis of CNN architectures (ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, GoogLeNet) to select MobileNetV2.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet dataset
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset used for pre-training the base MobileNetV2 model before transfer learning was applied to the skin lesion task.

use.training: Used for pre-training the base MobileNetV2 model to serve as the foundation for transfer learning.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

ISIC 2024 dataset.notes: The MobileNetV2 model was selected from a comparative analysis of ResNet-18, EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, and GoogLeNet, achieving the highest recall (99.2%) and accuracy (97.7%) during that specific evaluation phase. The final deployed model on the Raspberry Pi achieved 96.4% accuracy and 98.5% recall on the test set. Validation accuracy stabilized at 98.9%.

Real-time Inference Test (Benign Skin An.notes: A real-world test was conducted using a benign skin anomaly (a pimple) uploaded via the web application on a Raspberry Pi. The system correctly identified it as non-cancerous with a low confidence score for melanoma (6.13%).

Comparative Analysis with Related Approa.notes: The proposed MobileNetV2 system outperformed similar edge-compatible systems in terms of accuracy. Other systems lacked full offline capability or clinical interoperability.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset, then fine-tuned using transfer learning on the ISIC 2024 dataset.

architecture_details.optimization: Converted to TensorFlow Lite format for reduced computational costs and edge computing efficiency

preprocessing: Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, contrast adjustments)
training_details.training_environment: Cloud-based GPU environment provided by Kaggle

classification_or_output_head.details: Final classification layers were trained on the ISIC 2024 dataset for binary classification (benign vs. malignant/melanoma). Output includes class labels and confidence scores.

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel value normalization (scaling to a consistent range)
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, contrast adjustments)
Class balancing using SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, flipping, and zooming were applied to mitigate overfitting and enhance model robustness.
Contrast adjustments were applied to mitigate overfitting and enhance model robustness.
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) was employed to address class imbalance by generating synthetic data points for underrepresented classes.

conclusions: The proposed AI and IoT system, utilizing a lightweight MobileNetV2 Convolutional Neural Network deployed on a Raspberry Pi edge device, achieved high diagnostic performance with 96.4% accuracy and 98.5% recall for skin cancer classification.

The system demonstrated robust generalization capabilities, with validation accuracy stabilizing at 98.9% and an ROC-AUC of 97.4%, indicating proficiency in distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions.

Real-time inference testing on a benign skin anomaly (a pimple) confirmed the system's reliability, correctly returning a low-confidence melanoma prediction (6.13%), which aligns with the non-cancerous nature of the lesion.

The MobileNetV2-based edge solution outperformed similar embedded AI systems in terms of accuracy (96.4%) compared to custom CNNs (92.8%), VGG16 (94.6%), and cloud-based ResNet-50 (95.1%) approaches, while maintaining computational efficiency.

The integration of the AI model with a Flask-based web application and MQTT protocol enables secure data transmission, Electronic Health Record (EHR) interoperability via HL7 and FHIR standards, and offline capabilities suitable for Hospital 4.0 environments.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the ISIC dataset's potential lack of global skin tone diversity, which may affect model generalizability across all patient demographics.

Hardware constraints of the Raspberry Pi limit the use of more advanced deep learning models or high-resolution imagery, suggesting future work should focus on dataset expansion, enhanced UI/UX, and optional cloud-based model updates.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the ISIC 2024 dataset may lack global skin tone diversity, which poses a limitation for model generalizability across all patient demographics.
Hardware constraints of the Raspberry Pi limit the use of more advanced deep learning models or high-resolution imagery, restricting the system's computational ceiling.

The system currently lacks implemented functionalities for cloud-based predictive analytics, centralized model updates, long-term data storage, and full integration with Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems, despite claiming interoperability standards.
The real-time inference validation was performed on a single benign skin anomaly (a pimple) via a self-administered test, which provides limited evidence of robustness in diverse clinical scenarios.
The paper is a conference publication (ICCSC 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal article, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal standards.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics only.

The model was trained on the ISIC 2024 dataset, which is a public benchmark; external validation on independent, multi-center clinical datasets is not reported, raising concerns about real-world clinical applicability.
